section: Mechanical properties of Fe–Al–M–C (MZTi, V, Nb, Ta) alloys with strengthening carbides and Laves phase

context_update.summary: This first chunk covers the paper's abstract, introduction, microstructure summary (Table 1), and experimental procedures. The study investigates Fe-15Al and Fe-26Al based alloys with additions of 1 at.% C and 2 or 4 at.% Ti, V, Nb, Ta. The Fe-15Al alloys have an A2 disordered bcc matrix, while the Fe-26Al alloys undergo D03/B2 (~550 °C) and B2/A2 (~800 °C) order-disorder transitions. The microstructure details from Table 1 reveal grain morphologies (polygonal, columnar, dendritic, zig-zag) and sizes, and precipitate types (MC carbides and Laves phase) with their morphologies and sizes. For instance, Fe-15Al-2Ti-1C shows a dendritic matrix with fibrous lamellar TiC eutectic, and Fe-26Al-4Nb-1C contains both NbC and plate-like (Fe,Al)2Nb Laves phase. Mechanical testing includes hardness (Brinell, 62.5 g/2.5 mm), compression (6×6×10 mm³), and tension (3.5×5 mm², 15 mm gauge length) at a strain rate of 10⁻⁴ s⁻¹. Elevated-temperature tests use a 30 min hold in argon with continuous thermocouple monitoring. Fracture surfaces are examined by SEM (20 kV, 15 mm WD, SE). Subsequent chunks will present mechanical property data (hardness, yield stress, ductility) as a function of composition and temperature, and relate them to the microstructural features described here.
processing_route: copper mould casting (32 mm diameter × 186 mm length)
tensile specimens 3.5×5 mm², 15 mm gauge length
compression and tension tests at strain rate 10⁻⁴ s⁻¹
elevated-temperature testing: 30 min hold, argon atmosphere, thermocouple on surface
SEM fractography (20 kV, 15 mm WD, secondary electrons)
key_variables: alloying element type (Ti, V, Nb, Ta)
testing temperature (room temperature to elevated temperatures, including yield stress anomaly range)
microstructure: Fe-15Al: A2 disordered bcc, 500 μm polygonal grains, no precipitates
Fe-15Al-2Ti-1C: dendritic matrix, equiaxed/columnar 30/30×80 μm, TiC fibrous lamellar (eutectic) 1.5 μm
Fe-15Al-2V-1C: columnar matrix 350×2000 μm, VC plate-like (needle-like) 1×8 μm
Fe-15Al-2Nb-1C: dendritic matrix, equiaxed/columnar 30/30×80 μm, NbC granular lamellar (eutectic) <1 μm
Fe-15Al-2Ta-1C: dendritic matrix, equiaxed/columnar 30/30×80 μm, TaC fibrous lamellar (eutectic) <1 μm
Fe-26Al: D03/B2 ~550 °C, B2/A2 ~800 °C, 700 μm polygonal grains, no precipitates
Fe-26Al-2Ti-1C: polygonal grains 120 μm, TiC cube-like 2 μm
Fe-26Al-4Ti-1C: polygonal grains 60 μm, TiC cube-like 2 μm
Fe-26Al-2V-1C: columnar grains 450×3000 μm, VC plate-like (needle-like) 0.5×15 μm
Fe-26Al-2Nb-1C: zig-zag grains 200 μm, NbC cube-like 2.5 μm / script-like (eutectic) 1×5 μm
Fe-26Al-4Nb-1C: polygonal grains 60 μm, NbC cube-like 1.8 μm, (Fe,Al)2Nb Laves phase plate-like 2×5 μm
Fe-26Al-2Ta-1C: polygonal and partially zig-zag grains 200 μm, TaC cube-like 5 μm / script-like 1 μm
mechanisms: order-disorder transitions (D03/B2, B2/A2) are expected to influence mechanical behavior
unresolved_terms: specific mechanical property values (to be provided in subsequent sections)

context_update.summary: This chunk describes experimental procedures (hardness, compression/tension specimen dimensions, strain rate, elevated-temperature setup, SEM fractography) and reports extensive room-temperature mechanical properties for Fe–15Al and Fe–26Al based alloys with 1 at.% C and 2 or 4 at.% Ti, V, Nb, Ta. Hardness (HB) and compression yield stress are presented in Fig. 1 and Table 2; Fe–15Al–2M–1C alloys all strengthen relative to binary Fe–15Al, while Fe–26Al based alloys with Ti soften. Engineering stress–strain curves (Figs 2 and 3) reveal parabolic work hardening for Fe–15Al–2M–1C and constant-then-parabolic hardening for Fe–26Al alloys. Tension–compression asymmetry is observed (compressive yield > tensile yield), notably 36% for Fe–26Al–2V–1C. Fractography (Fig. 4) shows transgranular cleavage, intergranular decohesion, and precipitate effects on fracture paths. Table 2 summarizes hardness, yield stresses, elongation, and fracture mode for all alloys. Fig. 5 gives elevated-temperature compression yield stress for Fe–15Al based alloys up to 800 °C. Later sections will likely discuss high-temperature properties and mechanisms in Fe–26Al alloys and yield stress anomaly.
processing_route: tensile specimens 3.5×5 mm² cross-section, 15 mm gauge length
elevated temperature testing in argon, 30 min hold
key_variables: alloying element type (Ti, V, Nb, Ta)
testing temperature (room temperature, and 400–800 °C for Fe-15Al alloys)
precipitate type and morphology (MC carbides, Laves phase)
microstructure: transgranular cleavage (fine or coarse facets) in most alloys
intergranular decohesion in Fe-26Al-2Ti-1C, Fe-26Al-4Ti-1C, Fe-26Al-2Ta-1C, Fe-26Al-4Nb-1C
cube-like TiC precipitates on intergranular facets of Fe-26Al-4Ti-1C
plate-like VC precipitates in Fe-26Al-2V-1C controlling fracture facet orientation (001)planes
properties: hardness: Fe-15Al 159 HB to Fe-15Al-2Nb-1C 309 HB
compression yield stress: Fe-15Al 356 MPa to Fe-15Al-2Ta-1C 868 MPa; Fe-26Al 644 MPa, Fe-26Al-4Nb-1C 792 MPa
tensile yield stress and total elongation: Fe-15Al-2Ti-1C 420 MPa, 3.3%; Fe-26Al-2Nb-1C 427 MPa, 1.4%
elevated temperature compression yield stress for Fe-15Al based alloys: steep drop above 700 °C
mechanisms: parabolic work hardening in Fe-15Al based alloys
constant then parabolic hardening in Fe-26Al based alloys
tension-compression asymmetry due to nonplanar screw dislocation core, Bauschinger effect, and precipitate notch sensitivity
precipitate crystallography influences fracture path (e.g., VC (001) planes)

context_update.summary: Section 3.2 reports high-temperature compression yield stress for Fe-15Al and Fe-26Al based alloys up to 800 °C. Fe-15Al alloys show monotonic decrease, with Nb/Ta variants strongest (Figure 5). Fe-26Al alloys exhibit a yield stress anomaly: Ti/V peak ≈550 °C, 400 MPa; Nb/Ta shift peak to lower temperatures; Fe-26Al-4Nb-1C peaks at 474 °C, 619 MPa, demonstrating Laves phase strengthening (Figure 6). Tension tests at 650 °C and 800 °C confirm tension-compression asymmetry for all alloys (Figures 7, 8). Summary and conclusions (Section 4) restates room-temperature ductility maxima (Fe-15Al-2Ti-1C: 3.3%, Fe-26Al-2Nb-1C: 1.4%), fracture modes (TGC, IGD), and highlights promising properties of Nb/Ta + C alloys. Subsequent chunks will likely link these properties to microstructural features described earlier, especially precipitate types and the role of D03/B2 order-disorder transitions in the yield stress anomaly.
key_variables: alloy composition (Ti, V, Nb, Ta content)
mechanisms: precipitation strengthening by MC carbides and Laves phase
yield stress anomaly related to order-disorder transitions
